title: Executive Order 11911—Preservation of Endangered Species

flags.justification: The Secretary of the Interior's Management Authority role is checked by a separate, six-agency Scientific Authority body and an explicit requirement to consult with other agencies (Sec. 2).
Clear, specific role division between the Management Authority and the Scientific Authority, with defined consultation requirements.
Multiple related functions (treaty communication, scientific advice, permit standardization) all coherently serve one purpose: endangered species treaty implementation.
This assigns administrative and coordination roles for treaty implementation; it does not itself create a detailed compliance regime against private parties.
No compliance regime exists for an incentive structure to attach to.
A standing treaty-implementation and environmental-protection framework, evergreen by nature.
Sec. 2's requirement to remain consistent with other agencies' legally vested functions is protective, not oversight-removing.

Sec. 3(e) requires the Scientific Authority to ascertain the views of state agencies, humane groups, zoological and botanical institutions, and commercial and conservation interests — a genuinely broad, real external-input requirement.
Establishes the durable, still-operating U.S. institutional framework for implementing CITES, an internationally significant wildlife-trade treaty.
A carefully designed, six-agency Scientific Authority with clearly defined, non-overlapping representative roles.
No coverage population or exemption structure is created.

notes: Validation batch (positive). Structural weight: 1/16 = 6.25%. Establishes the U.S. institutional framework for CITES implementation, still substantially in effect today.
